# ainative — Companion Software for AI Native Business

> Companion software for the AI Native Business book by Manav Sehgal. A personal research project exploring what it takes to build AI-native businesses on a shoestring. Local-first, open source, free.

URL: https://ainative.business
License: Apache 2.0
Status: Pre-alpha (active development)
Author: Manav Sehgal
GitHub: https://github.com/manavsehgal/ainative

---

## Project Overview

ainative is the companion software to the AI Native Business book — a 14-chapter playbook for building autonomous business systems as a solo or small-team operator. The book lays out the principles; ainative is the working scaffold readers can study, run locally, and extend.

The runtime, governance model, and local-first architecture are unchanged from prior iterations of this research project. What's new is the framing: ainative now exists in service of the book, as a builder scaffold for AI-native businesses rather than a standalone product pitch.

### What ainative Provides

- A goal-oriented, persistent, memory-native multi-agent execution scaffold readers can install, inspect, and modify.
- A reference implementation of the architectural patterns described in the book — task graphs, hierarchical memory, graduated autonomy, multi-model routing.
- A local-first runtime (Tauri-based desktop) with cloud-optional reach, so readers can run the same task graph on their machine or push it to elastic compute.

### Five Differentiation Pillars

1. **Long-Horizon Task Persistence** — Tasks that span hours, days, or weeks. Checkpoint/resume, progress tracking, failure recovery, and resource budgets. Task persistence is the architectural foundation, not a feature bolted onto chat.

2. **Multi-Model Orchestration** — Route subtasks to the best available model. Claude for reasoning, GPT for long-context, Gemini for research, Grok for speed, open-source models for cost control and privacy. Transparent routing with measured performance — including local model support via Ollama for offline work.

3. **Memory-Native Architecture** — Memory as a core primitive, not a subsystem. Four-tier hierarchical memory: working (active context), episodic (past interactions), semantic (distilled knowledge), and procedural (learned strategies). Agents curate their own memory — not just passively accumulate it.

4. **Graduated Autonomy** — Trust calibrated by observed performance. New or risky tasks run in high-oversight mode. Well-understood tasks graduate to autonomous execution. Trust is per-agent-type, per-task-type, and per-risk-level — not global. Hard boundaries remain regardless of trust level.

5. **Hybrid Execution** — Local-first with cloud-optional elasticity. Tauri-based desktop application for privacy, low latency, and full filesystem access. Cloud-optional for background execution and elastic compute. The same task graph runs locally or in the cloud — the orchestration layer is location-agnostic.

### Architecture

Rust + TypeScript hybrid. Rust owns the system — desktop shell, WASM sandboxing, persistent storage, IPC. TypeScript owns the intelligence — LLM interaction, agent logic, tool execution, protocol communication. Neither crosses into the other's domain.

The boundary is architectural, not arbitrary. Rust's safety and performance handle the system-level concerns that agents must never compromise. TypeScript's ecosystem breadth provides access to every major LLM SDK and the growing MCP connector ecosystem.

Six protocols form the communication backbone:
- MCP (Agent ↔ Tool): Tool invocation, resource access, connector ecosystem
- A2A (Agent ↔ Agent): Task delegation, capability discovery, result sharing
- CDP (Agent ↔ Browser): DOM interaction, navigation, screenshot capture
- WebMCP (Agent ↔ Website): Structured web data access (preferred over CDP)
- Tauri IPC (Frontend ↔ Backend): Task graph operations, agent lifecycle, settings
- WebSocket (Frontend ↔ Agent): Real-time output streaming, tool call events

Technology: Rust, TypeScript, Tauri, React, SQLite, WASM, MCP, A2A, WebMCP, CDP, Ollama

---

## The Book: AI Native Business

AI Native Business is a 14-chapter playbook for building autonomous business systems on a shoestring. It is written for solo builders and small teams who want to compose AI agents into a working business — across marketing, sales, operations, and product — without hiring an army or signing up for a cloud lock-in.

The book pairs with the ainative companion software so readers can move from principle to practice in the same session: read a chapter, open the local runtime, run the example.

Full chapter content lives at https://ainative.business/book/. Each chapter is published as a standalone page with code, diagrams, and citations.

### Themes Covered

- The shape of an AI-native business: what changes when agents are first-class operators rather than tools.
- Local-first architecture for solo builders: privacy, latency, cost control, and the freedom to leave any vendor.
- Multi-model routing as a cost and capability strategy: choosing the right model per subtask, measuring what works.
- Memory as the substrate of compounding leverage: four-tier hierarchical memory and hybrid retrieval.
- Graduated autonomy as a governance model: how trust is earned per-agent, per-task, per-risk.
- Hybrid execution: when to keep work local, when to push it to the cloud, and how to keep the task graph portable.
- Building, observing, and auditing autonomous workflows that span hours, days, or weeks.

For the canonical chapter list and current revision, see https://ainative.business/book/.

---

## Research

The Research section at https://ainative.business/research/ collects supporting work — the solo-builder case study, the architectural rationale behind ainative, and the multi-year project portfolio that informed the book.

### Solo-Builder Context

The research portfolio behind this work spans nine projects across five technology waves: 270K+ lines of code, 30+ AI agents built, 8 production systems, 18+ projects shipped, and 68+ blog articles. ainative is the synthesis of what worked across that portfolio.

Representative projects: FinEdge (fintech, 45K LOC), SuperCRM (social CRM, 35.7K LOC), KnowledgeGraph (strategic intelligence, 81.7K LOC), AgentKit (visual agent testing, 473 tests), Canvas OS (analytics, 28.4K LOC), DeepResearch (RAG, 36 blueprints), BrowseAI (browser extension, 21.9K LOC), TerminalOS (CLI automation, 15K LOC), ainative (companion runtime, 7.3K+ LOC).

### Architectural Rationale

The architectural pillars below are the same ones described throughout the book — restated here for readers who arrive via the runtime rather than the book.

**Long-Horizon Task Persistence** — Tasks that span hours, days, or weeks require fundamentally different architecture than chat-based interactions. Checkpoint/resume, progress tracking across task graphs, failure recovery with automatic retry and escalation, per-task resource budgets.

**Multi-Model Orchestration** — Different models excel at different capabilities. Transparent routing with measured performance. Local model support via Ollama for offline operation.

**Memory-Native Architecture** — Four-tier hierarchical memory: Working (active task context), Episodic (session history), Semantic (cross-session distilled knowledge), Procedural (accumulated learned strategies). Hybrid retrieval: BM25 + vector similarity + MMR reranking.

**Graduated Autonomy** — Three trust levels: Supervised (new/risky tasks, plan-and-review), Semi-autonomous (demonstrated competence, periodic checkpoints), Autonomous (proven track record, post-hoc review). Trust scoped per-agent-type, per-task-type, per-risk-level.

**Hybrid Execution** — Tauri-based desktop app. Local-first with cloud-optional. Same task graph runs locally or in the cloud. State portability between environments.

### Technology Stack Detail

Rust + TypeScript Hybrid:
- Rust (Tauri): WASM sandboxing, SQLite storage, IPC, memory safety
- TypeScript: LLM SDK integration, MCP communication, protocol handling

Protocol Stack:
- MCP: Agent ↔ Tool communication
- A2A: Agent ↔ Agent communication
- CDP: Agent ↔ Browser interaction
- WebMCP: Structured web data access
- Tauri IPC: Frontend ↔ Backend operations
- WebSocket: Real-time streaming

Sandboxing: WASM sandboxes (Wasmtime) for lightweight, formally isolated execution. Docker/cloud sandboxes as fallbacks for heavyweight operations.

---

## Status and Roadmap

ainative is pre-alpha and under active development as the book is written. The runtime evolves alongside the chapters; expect both to change frequently.

Phase 1 Foundation: Working MVP — Tauri shell, task canvas, single-model agent, MCP tools, basic memory.
Phase 2 Distribution: Reader-installable release — cross-platform, community templates, contributor infra.
Phase 3 Maturity: Tier 2 features — agent inspector, browser fleet, connector marketplace, semantic memory.
Phase 4 Ecosystem: Platform growth — cloud sandbox integration, A2A protocol, community ecosystem.

The long-term vision: an open scaffold for AI-native businesses that any solo builder can read, run, and extend — paired with a book that explains why the pieces fit together the way they do.

---

## References

1. Anthropic, "Model Context Protocol Specification," 2024-2026.
2. Google, "Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A) Specification," 2025.
3. Google, "WebMCP: Structured Agent-Web Interaction Protocol," 2025.
4. Tauri Contributors, "Tauri 2.0 Architecture Documentation," 2024-2025.
5. Bytecode Alliance, "Wasmtime: A Fast and Secure Runtime for WebAssembly," 2024.
6. React Flow Contributors, "React Flow: Wire Your Ideas with React Flow," 2024.
7. SQLite Consortium, "sqlite-vec: Vector Search Extension for SQLite," 2024.
8. Robertson, S., "BM25 and Beyond," Foundations and Trends in IR, 2009.
9. Carbonell, J. and Goldstein, J., "MMR Diversity-Based Reranking," SIGIR, 1998.
10. OpenTelemetry Contributors, "OpenTelemetry Specification," 2024.
11. W3C, "WASI Preview 2 Specification," 2025.
12. Research portfolio analysis, 2026.
